past_medical_history: Intermittent asthma since childhood, well-controlled with rescue inhaler
Hypothyroidism diagnosed 2 years ago, on stable levothyroxine
No prior stroke, TIA, or known vascular disease
family_history: No family history of early stroke, connective tissue disorders, or coagulation abnormalities

social_history.alcohol: Social, 1-2 glasses of wine per week
social_history.occupation: Recreational volleyball player and graduate student in biology
social_history.living_situation: Lives with partner in an apartment
social_history.exercise: Active, plays volleyball 3 times per week and jogs regularly

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Mildly anxious. Speech is fluent but with occasional word-finding pauses. Follows complex two-step commands without difficulty. No frank aphasia but subtle expressive language difficulty noted on detailed testing. No neglect on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, equal and reactive to light. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. No visual field deficit on confrontation. Facial sensation intact to light touch bilaterally. No facial asymmetry at rest; subtle right lower facial weakness noted on attempted smile. Hearing intact bilaterally to finger rub. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength normal bilaterally.
neurological_exam.motor: Right upper extremity: grip strength mildly reduced at 4/5, finger extension 4+/5, deltoid and biceps 5/5. Right lower extremity: 5/5 throughout. Left upper and lower extremities: 5/5 throughout. No pronator drift on sustained arm extension, though right hand shows subtle fine motor slowing on rapid finger tapping.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch, pinprick, and temperature bilaterally. Proprioception intact in all four extremities. No extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric in upper and lower extremities bilaterally. Plantar responses are flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Rapid alternating movements slightly slow on the right compared to left. Heel-to-shin normal bilaterally.
neurological_exam.gait: Ambulates independently with normal stride and base. No ataxia. Tandem gait slightly unsteady but performed.
neurological_exam.additional: NIHSS score: 3. No meningismus. No cervical bruits auscultated. Mild tenderness over the left lateral neck on palpation. No Horner syndrome.

patient.chief_complaint: Episodic right hand clenching and difficulty with speech and fine hand movements
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Sofia Delgado is a 28-year-old right-handed woman who presents to the emergency department after experiencing a second episode of involuntary right hand clenching followed by transient difficulty with speech and fine motor movements in her right hand. Today's episode occurred approximately 2 hours prior to arrival while she was studying at home. She noted a sudden involuntary tonic posturing of her right hand lasting approximately 30 seconds, followed by difficulty typing on her laptop and mild slurring of her speech that persisted for roughly 15 minutes before gradually improving. She reports a similar but briefer episode approximately 2 weeks ago while at volleyball practice, which she initially attributed to muscle cramps and fatigue and did not seek medical attention. Notably, approximately 3 weeks prior to today's presentation, she sustained a direct impact to the left side of her neck from a volleyball during practice. She experienced mild neck pain and stiffness for a few days afterward but did not seek medical evaluation as the pain resolved with over-the-counter analgesics. She denies any headache, visual changes, nausea, vomiting, loss of consciousness, or generalized seizure activity during either episode. She has no history of seizures or epilepsy. She denies any recent febrile illness, weight loss, night sweats, skin rashes, or joint pain. She has not traveled recently and has no history of trauma other than the volleyball incident. Her partner, who witnessed today's episode, confirms the description and notes that she appeared fully conscious throughout, though she seemed frustrated by her difficulty speaking. The patient is particularly concerned because she needs full use of her right hand for laboratory work in her graduate program.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric and reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Not captured during this routine recording

findings.morphology: High-voltage polymorphic delta waves, intermittently semi-rhythmic
findings.state: Present during wakefulness and persists during hyperventilation

findings.morphology: Sharp contour with subsequent slow wave, phase-reversal at C3

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Augmented the left frontocentral slowing; no definite epileptiform discharges elicited
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response; no photoparoxysmal response

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG due to subcontinuous left frontocentral focal slowing with occasional sharp transients in the left central region. Occasional sharp transients may represent secondary cortical irritability. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 30-minute recording; prolonged EEG monitoring may be warranted to better characterize the sharp transients and assess for subclinical seizure activity.

findings.location: Left frontoparietal cortex and subcortical white matter, extending to involve the body of the corpus callosum on the left side
findings.size: Approximately 3.5 x 2.2 cm in maximal axial dimensions

signal_characteristics.ADC: Corresponding hypointensity confirming true restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense signal consistent with subacute ischemic changes
signal_characteristics.T1: Subtle hypointensity within the affected territory
signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense signal in the left frontoparietal region

findings.mass_effect: No significant mass effect. No midline shift.
findings.borders: Margins do not conform neatly to a single major vascular territory; distribution involves left MCA-ACA watershed zone

signal_characteristics.DWI: Mildly hyperintense, less bright than frontoparietal lesion
signal_characteristics.ADC: Mildly hypointense, suggesting late subacute phase

additional_observations: No evidence of acute or chronic hemorrhage in the brain parenchyma or subarachnoid space
No enhancing mass lesion identified on post-contrast sequences
No evidence of cerebral venous sinus thrombosis on post-contrast images
Incidental 4 mm pineal cyst, likely benign
mri.impression: Late subacute ischemic lesion in the left globus pallidus, likely slightly older than the frontoparietal lesion. No hemorrhage or enhancing mass. Incidental pineal cyst. The presence of multi-territory infarctions of differing ages in a young patient without traditional vascular risk factors warrants vascular imaging to evaluate for arterial dissection, vasculitis, or embolic source. Clinical correlation recommended.

ecg.interpretation: Normal 12-lead electrocardiogram. Normal sinus rhythm at 80 bpm with normal intervals and axis. No evidence of structural heart disease, arrhythmia, or conduction abnormalities.

abnormal_values_summary: TSH 5.8 mIU/L (mildly elevated, suboptimal thyroid replacement)

findings.location: Left internal carotid artery, cervical segment extending to the petrous segment
findings.size: Approximately 3 cm in craniocaudal length of dissection flap
findings.description: CTA: Eccentric mural thickening with intimal flap in the left ICA. Severe luminal narrowing (>70% stenosis) of the left ICA from approximately C2 vertebral level to the skull base. Crescent-shaped mural hematoma seen as hyperdensity within the vessel wall on non-contrast images.

findings.location: Left middle cerebral artery, M1 and proximal M2 segments
findings.description: CTA: Irregular caliber and thinning of the left MCA M1 segment with subtle beading pattern. Reduced opacification of the proximal M2 branches compared to the right side. No complete occlusion.

additional_observations: Right internal carotid artery is normal in caliber and morphology
Bilateral vertebral arteries are patent without evidence of dissection
Basilar artery is patent with normal caliber
Circle of Willis demonstrates bilateral posterior communicating arteries providing some collateral flow to the left hemisphere
output.impression: Secondary irregularity and thinning of the left MCA, likely representing distal thromboembolic sequelae or flow-related changes from the proximal dissection. No contralateral or vertebrobasilar dissection. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Interatrial Septum: No atrial septal defect identified at rest

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Lupus Anticoagulant Weakly positive  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Lupus anticoagulant: weakly positive (may be transient; repeat in 12 weeks) (H)

findings.region: Left internal carotid artery, mid-cervical segment
findings.signal: Peak_systolic_velocity: Elevated at 280 cm/s in the left ICA; End_diastolic_velocity: 105 cm/s; ICA_CCA_ratio: 4.2; Estimated_stenosis: Greater than 70% (NASCET criteria); B_mode: Eccentric intimal flap visualized; tapering of the vessel lumen with residual flow on color Doppler

output.impression: Normal right carotid and bilateral vertebral arteries. Findings corroborate the CTA diagnosis of left ICA dissection. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: cervical artery dissection treatment anticoagulation versus antiplatelet young stroke

results.title: Cervical Artery Dissection in Stroke Study (CADISS Trial)
results.authors: Markus HS, Hayter E, et al.
results.key_finding: No significant difference between antiplatelet and anticoagulant therapy for preventing stroke and death in patients with cervical artery dissection. Recurrent stroke rate was low in both groups.

results.title: Cervical artery dissection: Current concepts and management
results.key_finding: Cervical artery dissection accounts for approximately 20% of ischemic strokes in young adults. Trauma (including minor or sports-related) is a recognized precipitant. Antithrombotic therapy is recommended for at least 3-6 months.

results.title: AHA/ASA Scientific Statement on Cervical Artery Dissections
results.authors: Biller J, Sacco RL, et al.
results.key_finding: For extracranial carotid or vertebral artery dissection, antithrombotic therapy (antiplatelet or anticoagulation) for at least 3-6 months is reasonable. Screening for underlying connective tissue disorders is recommended.

output.summary: Current evidence from the CADISS trial shows no significant difference between anticoagulation and antiplatelet therapy for secondary stroke prevention in cervical artery dissection. Both are considered acceptable approaches. Antithrombotic therapy is recommended for 3-6 months with follow-up vascular imaging to assess for recanalization. Cervical artery dissection is a leading cause of stroke in young adults, and minor trauma (including sports injuries) is a recognized risk factor. Screening for connective tissue disorders (e.g., Ehlers-Danlos, Marfan, Loeys-Dietz syndromes) is recommended.

output.proposed: Warfarin or DOAC for cervical artery dissection; patient currently on combined oral contraceptive pill (ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel)
interactions: Warfarin + ethinyl estradiol: estrogen may reduce anticoagulant effect by increasing clotting factor synthesis (moderate interaction)
DOACs (apixaban, rivarfaxaban) + combined OCP: minimal direct pharmacokinetic interaction, but estrogen-containing OCP is an independent risk factor for thrombosis
contraindications: Estrogen-containing oral contraceptives are relatively contraindicated in patients with history of ischemic stroke or arterial thrombosis (WHO Medical Eligibility Criteria Category 4)
OCP should be discontinued given new diagnosis of ischemic stroke
warnings: Combined OCP use is an independent risk factor for both arterial and venous thrombosis, particularly in combination with other prothrombotic states
Progestin-only contraception or non-hormonal methods are safer alternatives in this patient
alternatives: For anticoagulation: aspirin 325 mg daily or aspirin 81 mg + clopidogrel as antiplatelet alternative to anticoagulation per CADISS data

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke secondary to left internal carotid artery dissection, likely traumatic

differential.key_distinguishing: EEG shows focal slowing consistent with structural lesion rather than primary epileptiform activity; MRI confirms ischemic infarction rather than epileptic focus
No evidence of hemorrhage on MRI in subarachnoid space or brain parenchyma
Normal echocardiogram, normal sinus rhythm on ECG; vascular imaging confirms carotid dissection as the embolic source
Normal inflammatory markers, negative ANA, negative ANCA, negative rheumatoid factor; no vessel wall enhancement on imaging
Lupus anticoagulant weakly positive but likely transient (OCP use); anticardiolipin and beta-2 glycoprotein antibodies negative; does not meet diagnostic criteria without confirmation at 12 weeks

differential.diagnosis: Connective tissue disorder (Ehlers-Danlos, Loeys-Dietz) predisposing to dissection
differential.key_distinguishing: No hypermobility, normal aortic root, genetic panel negative for COL3A1, FBN1, TGFBR1/2 mutations

optimal_actions.action: Perform urgent neurological assessment including NIHSS and detailed history focusing on timeline, trauma history, and risk factors for stroke in a young patient
optimal_actions.expected_finding: NIHSS 3, history of cervical trauma 3 weeks prior, recurrent transient neurological events

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to evaluate for possible epileptic etiology of the episodic symptoms
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Focal slowing in left frontocentral region suggesting structural lesion rather than primary epilepsy

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging to evaluate for acute ischemic lesion or other structural pathology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Acute-to-subacute ischemic infarction in left frontoparietal region with older lesion in left globus pallidus; no hemorrhage

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat labs including CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, and inflammatory markers to evaluate for systemic causes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal labs except mildly elevated TSH; normal inflammatory markers and coagulation studies

optimal_actions.action: Obtain ECG to evaluate for cardiac arrhythmia as potential embolic source
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal sinus rhythm, no arrhythmia identified

optimal_actions.action: Obtain CT angiography or MR angiography of head and neck to evaluate for arterial dissection given history of cervical trauma and multi-territory infarctions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left internal carotid artery dissection with severe stenosis and distal MCA irregularity

optimal_actions.action: Initiate antithrombotic therapy (anticoagulation or antiplatelet) for secondary stroke prevention in cervical artery dissection
Discontinue combined oral contraceptive pill given new diagnosis of ischemic stroke and discuss alternative contraception
optimal_actions.expected_finding: OCP contraindicated after ischemic stroke; progestin-only or non-hormonal alternatives recommended

optimal_actions.action: Order echocardiogram with bubble study to evaluate for cardiac source and PFO
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal echocardiogram, no structural cardiac abnormality

optimal_actions.action: Order hypercoagulability panel to evaluate for underlying thrombophilia
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Largely normal with transient weakly positive lupus anticoagulant

optimal_actions.action: Screen for connective tissue disorders to evaluate for predisposition to arterial dissection
Plan follow-up vascular imaging in 3-6 months to assess for recanalization of the dissected artery

critical_actions: Obtain brain MRI to confirm ischemic stroke and assess for multi-territory involvement
Obtain vascular imaging (CTA or MRA of head and neck) to diagnose carotid artery dissection
Identify the cervical trauma as a key historical detail linking to dissection etiology
Initiate antithrombotic therapy for secondary stroke prevention
Rule out alternative etiologies (epilepsy, vasculitis, hypercoagulable state, cardioembolic source)
contraindicated_actions: Administering IV thrombolysis (alteplase) when symptom onset is unclear or beyond the treatment window, and deficits are mild and improving
Continuing combined oral contraceptive pill in a patient with newly diagnosed ischemic stroke
Starting antiepileptic drugs as sole treatment without pursuing imaging workup for structural etiology
Performing aggressive anticoagulation with IV heparin in the acute phase without excluding hemorrhagic transformation
Dismissing the transient neurological episodes as benign without imaging evaluation
Performing lumbar puncture when clinical and imaging findings already suggest ischemic stroke etiology
key_reasoning_points: The episodic involuntary hand clenching and transient speech/motor difficulties in a young patient without epilepsy history should prompt evaluation for both seizure and vascular etiologies
EEG showing focal slowing without definite epileptiform activity suggests a structural lesion causing secondary cortical irritability rather than primary epilepsy
The history of cervical trauma from a volleyball impact 3 weeks prior is the critical diagnostic clue linking to carotid artery dissection

MRI showing multi-territory infarctions of differing ages (acute frontoparietal + late subacute pallidal) in a young patient without traditional vascular risk factors strongly suggests a proximal arterial source
Normal inflammatory markers, negative autoimmune panel, and negative connective tissue screening systematically exclude vasculitis and hereditary connective tissue disorders
The weakly positive lupus anticoagulant is likely transient (common with OCP use) and should not distract from the primary diagnosis of carotid dissection confirmed on CTA
The normal ECG and echocardiogram argue against a primary cardioembolic source, directing attention to the cervicocerebral vasculature
Discontinuation of the combined OCP is an important management step as estrogen-containing contraceptives are contraindicated after ischemic stroke

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate - young patient with atypical stroke presentation mimicking seizure; cervical trauma history is a key but non-obvious diagnostic clue; multiple differential diagnoses must be systematically excluded; incidental findings (mildly elevated TSH, weakly positive lupus anticoagulant) add confounding elements
